content_en:
The second dimension of the ideal Muslim's character, in al-Hashimi's framework, is his relationship with himself — the quality of his inner life, the management of his physical and psychological health, and the cultivation of the personal virtues that Islamic ethics emphasizes. This chapter reflects the Islamic principle that before a person can fulfill his obligations to others, he must first attend to his own spiritual and physical condition.

The ideal Muslim takes care of his body as a divine trust. The hadith 'Your body has a right over you' establishes an Islamic obligation of self-care that extends to adequate sleep, balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, and medical care when needed. Islam prohibits the neglect and abuse of the body just as it prohibits the opposite extreme of bodily obsession. The moderate path — maintaining the body in good health as the vessel through which divine service is performed — is the prophetic standard.

Personal cleanliness and hygiene are given extensive treatment, as they are in the Sunnah itself. The Prophet identified cleanliness (taharah) as half of faith, and the detailed prophetic guidance on personal hygiene — dental care, bathing, trimming nails, grooming of hair and beard — creates a standard of physical self-presentation that serves both personal dignity and social consideration. The ideal Muslim's person is clean, well-groomed, and pleasantly presented without vanity or excessive self-ornamentation.

The management of the inner life — controlling emotions, cultivating positive character, and combating negative impulses — is where al-Hashimi's treatment is most valuable. He surveys the Islamic virtues systematically: truthfulness (sidq), which the Prophet identified as leading to righteousness and righteousness as leading to Paradise; patience (sabr), which the Quran associates with divine companionship; humility (tawadu'), which the Prophet modeled in his personal conduct and described as elevating the humble in Allah's sight; and gratitude (shukr), which the Quran connects to divine increase in blessings.

The vices that the ideal Muslim strives to overcome are treated with equal specificity. Pride (kibr) is identified as the quality that led Iblis to ruin and that the Prophet warned against in the most emphatic terms. Envy (hasad) is described as 'eating' the good deeds of its possessor. Anger (ghadab) is addressed through the prophetic remedy of seeking refuge from Shaytan, sitting when standing, or making wudu when experiencing strong anger. These practical remedies for emotional vices reflect the Islamic understanding that character is not fixed but can be actively shaped through consistent practice.

Al-Hashimi also addresses the intellectual dimension of the Muslim's relationship with himself: the obligation to seek knowledge, to think carefully about matters of religion and worldly responsibility, and to avoid the intellectual laziness that accepts opinions without investigation. The Quran's repeated invitations to reflect, observe, and reason are not addressed to scholars only; they are addressed to every believer as an individual responsible for his own understanding of his religion and his world.
content_id:
Dimensi kedua dari karakter Muslim ideal, dalam kerangka al-Hasyimi, adalah hubungannya dengan dirinya sendiri — kualitas kehidupan batinnya, pengelolaan kesehatan fisik dan psikologisnya, dan pengembangan kebajikan-kebajikan pribadi yang ditekankan oleh etika Islam. Bab ini mencerminkan prinsip Islam bahwa sebelum seseorang dapat memenuhi kewajibannya kepada orang lain, ia harus terlebih dahulu memperhatikan kondisi spiritual dan fisiknya sendiri.

Muslim yang ideal merawat tubuhnya sebagai amanah ilahi. Hadis 'Tubuhmu memiliki hak atas dirimu' menetapkan kewajiban Islam untuk merawat diri yang mencakup tidur yang cukup, nutrisi yang seimbang, aktivitas fisik yang teratur, dan perawatan medis saat diperlukan. Islam melarang pengabaian dan penyalahgunaan tubuh sebagaimana juga melarang ekstrem yang sebaliknya — obsesi terhadap tubuh. Jalan tengah — menjaga tubuh dalam kondisi sehat sebagai wadah di mana ibadah kepada Allah dilakukan — adalah standar kenabian.

Kebersihan dan higienitas pribadi mendapatkan pembahasan yang luas, sebagaimana juga dalam Sunnah itu sendiri. Nabi ﷺ mengidentifikasi kebersihan (thaharah) sebagai separuh dari iman, dan panduan kenabian yang terperinci tentang higienitas pribadi — perawatan gigi, mandi, memotong kuku, perawatan rambut dan janggut — menciptakan standar penampilan fisik yang melayani baik martabat pribadi maupun pertimbangan sosial. Penampilan Muslim yang ideal bersih, terawat rapi, dan disajikan dengan menyenangkan tanpa kesombongan atau perhiasan diri yang berlebihan.

Pengelolaan kehidupan batin — mengendalikan emosi, mengembangkan karakter yang positif, dan memerangi dorongan-dorongan negatif — adalah di mana pembahasan al-Hasyimi paling berharga. Ia mengulas kebajikan-kebajikan Islam secara sistematis: kejujuran (shidq), yang oleh Nabi ﷺ diidentifikasikan sebagai menuju kepada kebaikan dan kebaikan menuju ke surga; kesabaran (shabr), yang oleh Al-Quran dikaitkan dengan kebersamaan ilahi; kerendahan hati (tawadhu'), yang dicontohkan oleh Nabi ﷺ dalam perilaku pribadinya dan digambarkan sebagai meninggikan yang rendah hati di hadapan Allah; dan rasa syukur (syukr), yang oleh Al-Quran dihubungkan dengan penambahan nikmat ilahi.

Kejahatan-kejahatan yang berupaya diatasi oleh Muslim yang ideal diperlakukan dengan kekhususan yang sama. Sombong (kibr) diidentifikasi sebagai sifat yang membawa Iblis kepada kehancuran dan yang paling keras diperingatkan oleh Nabi ﷺ. Iri hati (hasad) digambarkan sebagai 'memakan' amal-amal baik pemiliknya. Kemarahan (ghadab) diatasi melalui obat yang diajarkan Nabi ﷺ yaitu berlindung dari Setan, duduk ketika sedang berdiri, atau berwudu ketika merasakan kemarahan yang kuat. Obat-obat praktis untuk keburukan-keburukan emosional ini mencerminkan pemahaman Islam bahwa karakter tidak bersifat tetap tetapi dapat secara aktif dibentuk melalui praktik yang konsisten.

Al-Hasyimi juga membahas dimensi intelektual dari hubungan Muslim dengan dirinya sendiri: kewajiban mencari ilmu, berpikir dengan cermat tentang masalah-masalah agama dan tanggung jawab duniawi, dan menghindari kemalasan intelektual yang menerima pendapat tanpa penyelidikan. Undangan-undangan berulang Al-Quran untuk merenung, mengamati, dan bernalar tidak ditujukan kepada ulama saja; undangan itu ditujukan kepada setiap mukmin sebagai individu yang bertanggung jawab atas pemahamannya sendiri tentang agamanya dan dunianya.
content_ar:
The second dimension of the ideal Muslim's character, in al-Hashimi's framework, is his relationship with himself — the quality of his inner life, the management of his physical and psychological health, and the cultivation of the personal virtues that Islamic ethics emphasizes. This chapter reflects the Islamic principle that before a person can fulfill his obligations to others, he must first attend to his own spiritual and physical condition.

The ideal Muslim takes care of his body as a divine trust. The الحديث 'Your body has a right over you' establishes an Islamic obligation of self-care that extends to adequate sleep, balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, and medical care when needed. Islam prohibits the neglect and abuse of the body just as it prohibits the opposite extreme of bodily obsession. The moderate path — maintaining the body in good health as the vessel through which divine service is performed — is the prophetic standard.

Personal cleanliness and hygiene are given extensive treatment, as they are in the السنة النبوية itself. النبي ﷺ identified cleanliness (taharah) as half of faith, and the detailed prophetic guidance on personal hygiene — dental care, bathing, trimming nails, grooming of hair and beard — creates a standard of physical self-presentation that serves both personal dignity and social consideration. The ideal Muslim's person is clean, well-groomed, and pleasantly presented without vanity or excessive self-ornamentation.

The management of the inner life — controlling emotions, cultivating positive character, and combating negative impulses — is where al-Hashimi's treatment is most valuable. He surveys the Islamic virtues systematically: truthfulness (sidq), which النبي ﷺ identified as leading to righteousness and righteousness as leading to Paradise; patience (sabr), which the القرآن الكريم associates with divine companionship; humility (tawadu'), which النبي ﷺ modeled in his personal conduct and described as elevating the humble in الله's sight; and gratitude (shukr), which the القرآن الكريم connects to divine increase in blessings.

The vices that the ideal Muslim strives to overcome are treated with equal specificity. Pride (kibr) is identified as the quality that led Iblis to ruin and that النبي ﷺ warned against in the most emphatic terms. Envy (hasad) is described as 'eating' the good deeds of its possessor. Anger (ghadab) is addressed through the prophetic remedy of seeking refuge from Shaytan, sitting when standing, or making wudu when experiencing strong anger. These practical remedies for emotional vices reflect the Islamic understanding that character is not fixed but can be actively shaped through consistent practice.

Al-Hashimi also addresses the intellectual dimension of the Muslim's relationship with himself: the obligation to seek knowledge, to think carefully about matters of religion and worldly responsibility, and to avoid the intellectual laziness that accepts opinions without investigation. The القرآن الكريم's repeated invitations to reflect, observe, and reason are not addressed to scholars only; they are addressed to every believer as an individual responsible for his own understanding of his religion and his world.
